topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I clicked it open, my heart beginning a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. The subject line was stark: "Scholarship Application Update." My fingers hovered over the screen, then froze above the 'delete' button, unable to move. Slowly, I scrolled down, the words blurring as they swam before my eyes. 'Regrettably, your application has not been selected for funding at this time.' A strange, hollow sensation washed over me, starting in my chest and spreading outward. I reread the sentence three times, searching for a misplaced comma, a typo that could somehow reverse the outcome. When I finally looked up, the familiar glow of my laptop screen seemed too bright, the room suddenly too quiet. I stood up, pacing the small confines of my dorm room, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. My breath hitched in my throat, a dry, rasping sound.

[story 2]

I stared at the rejection letter as if it were a foreign language. The crisp white paper felt heavy in my hands, the ink seeming to mock my earnest hopes. My jaw tightened, muscles jumping beneath my skin. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to anchor myself, but the air felt thin. Every logical pathway in my mind stalled; the data didn't add up. I pressed the letter against my forehead for a moment, a strange, hot pressure blooming behind my eyes. When I finally lowered it, my hands were trembling so badly I had to grip the edge of my desk to steady them. A low murmur escaped my lips, a sound that was more a choked whisper than actual speech. I felt a dizzying lightness, as if the ground beneath me had suddenly tilted away.

[story 3]

It took a full minute before I registered the content of the notification. My eyes scanned the screen repeatedly, the text refusing to settle into comprehension. A slow, creeping numbness began to spread through my limbs, making it hard to feel the texture of the chair beneath me. I leaned back against the wall, the cool plaster offering no comfort. I blinked rapidly, trying to force the information into a different frame of reference, but it remained stubbornly out of reach. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me, loud enough to make me flinch. I dropped my phone onto the bedspread, the small sound echoing in the silence, and simply sat there, utterly still, processing the sudden, vast emptiness where expectation used to reside.
